item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's what I thought too - and then just decided to suspend my disbelief a little.

It's a limitation of the SCP format, that we've got to get these through Foundation files. In this case, what the author wants to do (i.e. the eggs telling Subject 18 that she's "sneezin' special") is worthwhile, and worth pushing the format a bit.

Yes, there's probably no way she'd write that down, or the Foundation wouldn't be curious about her afterward, but this way we get to hear it and imagine the what-if. If the alternative is cropping every scip text that is not very plausible, it'd suck all the joy out of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My thought is that she just didn't think it would work even with their help. She probably thought she would have a better time in the foundation if she did exactly what they wanted.

And if she really distrusts the foundation she might suspect it's all a setup and they know what she is hearing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Second thoughts many years later: why did Subject -18 write all that down?

The voices are telling her security codes, guard positions, that the powers-that-be are about to lock her up and throw away the key (at best); and she just keeps scribbling away with one of those multi-ink pens everyone thought were awesome in 4th grade.

It just doesn't fly. Even the most diligent stenographer or automatic-writer is going to at least attempt to leave the incriminating segments off the transcript.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"These SCP Foundation types would put you in chains if you so much as sneezed special."

Now I want there to be an SCP that sneezes anomalously, if there isn't one already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anyway, SCP-2140 requires there to be no less than three Daevite Cults still running around.

Those cults were retrocreated by 2140-1-D, the whole point of creating 2140 in the first place was to undo that, which presumably the Foundation did. The end result is that the people who were in those cults probably retroactively only included persons who had since died, unless the Foundation failed. Also Daevite cults (composed of human cultists) and actual Daeva are radically different things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I meant in the sense that SCPs and tales have come out that portray this idea as Something Mildly Inconceivable Due To Prior History. Anyway, SCP-2140 requires there to be no less than three Daevite Cults still running around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Nowadays" there's only one Daeva and he will probably use whatever means are effective at completing the Chronicles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reading the spoilers on the first page, it's kinda hilariously to think of the Daevites and Mekhanites being allies nowadays.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm proud to say I'm the 100th upvote for this marvelous SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The entities heavily reminded me of the 8 spells of the octavo from The Light Fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had actually assumed it was SCP 610 the flesh that hates, as it had previously been depicted as an enemy of the Church of the Broken God.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Finally coming across this I find that I really like a thorough and detailed report, and this is as information dense as it gets. It is a little complicated, yes, but not enough to be off-putting. In fact, I find it less complex than the political analyses I've been sifting for the past few days- and with less bickering. It hits all the connections in turn with a marksman's precision, from Buddhism to other SCPs (Daevites being my favorite), and while it's not emotionally stirring as some SCPs, I find it captures my interest sharply.

And that I think is more to the point: it intrigues me, and that is a set of SCPs that (while in the minority) are a necessity. Not everything has to be a rapist torture chair, otherwise the pull of such entities loses its grip. This one we know in some respects, but it's still confusing and dangerous so we cross our i's and dot our t's and load our guns and keep working. That's what we do here at the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like I should really love this. I like the idea of Buddhist Broken God cultists, but the execution just does nothing for me.

And maybe it's just me, but the bickering in experiment was much less funny than it was obnoxious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sane humans is actually what I was going for. They were once people, who reached CotBG-Nirvana, retreating entirely into a primordial state based on faith. They still work for and feel the religion, but they haven't yet realized that the veneer is fading…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel that the transcription of what the entities said detracts from the article, as they give the impression of being sane humans. If they gave the impression of being non-sane or non-human (or both) it would be better.

213 images of humans engaged in armed and unarmed combat with … fractal shapes.

The mental image of humans fighting fractals is amusing.

A single, genderless human transitioning, over the course of eleven illustrations, from a human, to a human composed partially of mechanical components, to a humanoid composed entirely of mechanical components, to a featureless ovoid figure.

The implication that the Church considers their paradise on Earth to be one where everyone is transformed into metallic ovoids is disturbing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love the re-imagining of the Cult going on here. I didn't realize before how the CotBG was so generically abrahamic. Big mono-God, what we should all serve and try to be more like, etc. The way buddist-esque elements are used to flesh out their philosophies makes them much more interesting.

I also like the implication that CotBG is a fractured, schismed, dysfunctional organization. It seems a fitting reflection of the nature of their deity.

item.title: I did not think of that
item.content:encoded:
…

/me clears throat

AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
uncontained/lost/destroyed fleshbeast-making SCP that is at least partially voluntary

How about the Unclean from 093?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's part of why I wrote this — there are strikingly few SCPs that give any sort of characterization to the Church, and I feel like each GOI needs a day in the limelight, so to speak.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Testing Log SCP-348-ALPHA-█

Subject: SCP-XXXX ("Billy Wondertainment"), recently injured during testing (moderate cut on left shoulder)
Brief Background: Unknown
Notes: Message appeared, Did I make you happy?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What are you talking about? Of course there's butter in my cake. I would never use a vague term like "shortening," it's not even in my comment.

:|

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not a kneejerk reaction though. It's like if someone made Billy Wondertainment, Dr. Wondertainment's son, an SCP. It would only be good considering GoI canon, and I'd downvote that too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, it's been a while since the last CotBG article.
I took me the comments to get it, mostly since I only read it once.
But after getting it, I like it immensely.
Have your +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, I don't remember the last time I saw an article that addressed the Church.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most indeed. What point is there arguing over containment/destruction when, if they spend their time bickering, there might not be anyone left to -have that argument-.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally see them working together when they both agree on a way to deal with something — that is, either they both think it should be contained rather than destroyed, or they both think it should be destroyed rather than contained. It would also work if something particularly important came about that needed their combined force to work against, and they could sort out whether to kill or contain it later.

Which of those, if any, is the best explanation for what goes on here is up to you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My head-canon has the GoC and Foundation being enemies only because of ideological differences in regards to protecting humanity. I can totally see them locking arms at the first sign of a threat that could end the human race or otherwise screw things up beyond all repair. Sort of a "enemies only because of technicalities" situation. Just my head-canon, mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm intrigued by this because it posits the Foundation working not just with the Church, but the GOC as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting juxtaposition of opinions on the use a GoI in an article, and of course, neither is wrong. However,

shortening

I'm sure there's a metaphor for the questionable conspicuous absence of butter in your cake, Rej….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Then explain this delicious cake I am eating whilst reading this article.

Backstory and worldbuilding are good. Kneejerk reactions to any attempts to actually do such things outside of the relative "safe zone" of tales that very few people will read, are not. I like this because it is so tied to the Church, and would like it less so if it was not as connected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: exhibit A isn't this scp

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OBJECTION!

Exhibit A is a damn tasty cake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I counter your counter with more food metaphors. Maybe I should get lunch.

Imagine you're baking a cake, except instead of being a cake, it's an article. There are two ingredients that you're focusing on: Flour and Frosting. You maintain that the use of the GoI in this cake is more like flour, because it's critical but still only part of the big picture. I don't think that GoIs should be used like frosting, because it's entirely superficial and wouldn't make much of a difference if it weren't there.

The use of the GoI in this SCP is not frosting, it's the whole cake. Everything else is the frosting. It relies far too heavily on the GoI, because without it, there's just about nothing. This isn't just flour, this is flour, eggs, sugar, salt, vanilla, butter, Quikngruvn's head, milk, and baking soda. It's too much.

Properly used, there's still an interesting SCP in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, in the third bullet point he is referred to as "Site Director Galluzzo", but in the footnote he is referred to as "Assistant Site Director Galluzzo" Not sure which one it is supposed to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I counter and say that I personally love articles like this one that give meat to the GOIs: I feel that the seasoning approach leads to a lot of "make a scip, throw in a GOI for shits and giggles" or "hey, we need a recovery log, let's use MC&D". Stuff like this has purpose behind it, that the GOI is not just thrown in there.

Properly used, the GOI should be part of the scip: not something extra sprinkled on top, but a critical ingredient in the story the article is trying to tell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like this because it uses the idea of a GoI in the wrong way.

Not that the CotBG is being misrepresented, but that the idea of a GoI shouldn't be the only meat to the article. If you subtract that, there's nothing left. Hell, 882 is still something minus the GoI (which it gets a free pass on anyways, because it made the GoI): a massive conglomeration of metal that makes people near it go crazy and try to feed it metal. That's a concept beyond the GoI.

Here, it's entirely CotBG. If you subtract that, you have six metal eggs that talk to you. There is nothing here that isn't specifically for adding backstory to a GoI. My opinion, and many other people's opinion, is this: GoIs should be like seasoning. They add flavor, but they aren't the whole meal. In this article, it's the whole meal, and it's not interesting enough to make up for the fact that (to continue the food metaphor) it's a big bowl of cinnamon.

Though I don't hate the concept, it adds little enough that I think it would be better as a tale and doesn't work as an SCP. So, downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks brah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this direction of the Church, and I think it's now part of my headcanon (though I like to think of them as being terribly fractured, since as long as the Foundation are holding parts of their God, no one can claim enough authority to unite the religion). Reading the descriptions of the stuff on the stones just made me like it even more. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made a tiny edit. "Passersby" is one word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Four humans with fog or smoke emitting from their mouths while their faces peel back. The emitted matter combines to form an indistinct fifth humanoid figure.

Did you not notice that? Or am I the only one who immediately thought of the 'Be like the Stars!' SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, I made the mistake of thinking that each egg was an individual person. I didn't even get half of those connections. I want to upvote this again now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love this. It's not my personal Church headcanon, but I really like it as its own canon. Maybe I'll write some stuff related to it, eventually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
HEVVY SHPOILERZ
...shpoilerz?

-3 consists of enemies of the Church of the Broken God:

  • Foundation, for containing their objects. What's behind the blackboxes is up to you.
  • GOC, for destroying their objects. The objects described are not any SCP articles, because they were contained by the GOC.
  • Dr. Wondertainment, who is tempting members with his shiny baubles and western materialism (if you couldn't guess, I have the CotBG as being heavily buddhism-inspired, so materialism = BAD).
  • The Fifthists, for being a competing religion.

-4 consists of friends/allies:

  • The Church had a hold of SCP-222 for a while, which is where a number of their servants came from. They miss it.
  • The Serpent's Hand. Anyone who's against the Foundation and GOC and who's all for letting anomalies do as they please is a friend of the Church.
  • AWCY? is already chock full of open-minded youth, who are prone to joining alternative religions. The coolists find inspiration and materials with the Church, and the Church gets recruits and a new perspective on their philosophy.
  • SCP-140 — The CotBG as presented here is a fusion of Buddhist and Daevite philosophy and religion, with a prophetic figure in their past. The Daevites are the blacked-out civilization. Thanks to their thaumaturgical abilities and warlike nature, Daevites made excellent generals.

Aside from the main sequence, the illustrations on -5 show the bad paths one can go down. In order, those are a currently uncontained/lost/destroyed fleshbeast-making SCP that is at least partially voluntary, the Fifthists again (and a more general warning about religions that preach that one's soul escapes up into heaven), and more fractals. The fractals could be a reasonably literal something the CotBG finds them turning into and facing, or something more symbolic.

For reference, 1, 2, and 6 are War, Peace, and Enigma respectively. -6 would have been nigh-impossible to be carved through normal means (both due to the math not existing and over two kilometers of engravings, so that's reasonably solid evidence that these were created with the images already on them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to do with this what I do with every SCP I like: find obscure ways to tie it into the Fifth Church, and then upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've stated as much in chat, but this wasn't inspired by Hellboy even a little. Glad that it reminded you of something you liked, though :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I really like the writing, and the amount of thought put in to this, I can't upvote it.

The containment procedures are over-complicated, and feels like the foundation couldn't be bothered to actually contain these things. On top of that, we have members from a group of interest that actively stymies the foundation regularly, working with this object. That, to me, is a *massive* security hole, and could not end well.

I love the writing, but it just doesn't feel like a foundation document to me. Feels like a CotBG thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All of those are correct, although:

stuff
stuff

It could be 217 or, like the Buddhist Nirvana, is a result of extensive meditation and contemplation. It's not impossible that another one of these will spontaneously arise, possibly even from one of the worshipers around -6. The Foundation has suspected this, and has made leeway in the types of sustenance allowed for those allowed around them in case they start turning into clocks somewhere along the line.

You definitely seem to get it. And yes, that was a Telekill reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hellboy, your fall should be like the fall of mountains. But I was before mountains. I was in the beginning, and shall be forever. The first and the last. The world come full circle. You think you can fight and kill me, as you would a beast? I am not the wheel. I am the hand that turns the wheel. I am time, the destroyer. I was the wind and the stars before this. Before planets, before heaven and hell. And when all's done, I will be wind again, to blow this world as dust back into endless space. To me, the coming and going of man is as nothing. And you, because you made this choice to live like a man, in the end you also will be nothing. Here now, with me, this is your only moment, your glory.

Yeah. This reminds me very much of the Ogdru Jahad. For some reason that just makes me love it even more. Great show.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, you know what? I didn't like it the first two times I read it, but maybe it's just grown on me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The voices would probably be as old as the eggs and the eggs are old so… guessing they are old.

The implication from the speech though is that they aren't wholly independent of the listener and relies on them to some extent.

So lots of references and hidden subtexts here, which are always a bonus. I'm missing one reference on SCP-1564-3, and I'm not sure on the symbols on -4 and -5 enough to claim I know what they are for sure.

For the actual church expansion, I like the idea of them just being crazy and forming a cult based on that. The trouble has always been how they advanced into the whole Broken God concept. I don't think I have an issue with it coming from psychic clockwork eggs, so sure. Why not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's funny that 1564-7, being so old as I'm understanding it, is still so petty and bitchy with itself. I could imagine that over time this sort of behavior would develop but given even MORE time I'd think that it would fade away, giving rise to something more defeated or perhaps even serene…detached? I thought their 'conversation' was funny (and I loved the color idea) but it just ruined the article a little for me, even though I upvoted.

If I'm misunderstanding and 1564-7 isn't really all that old then never mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let's see if I get this correctly.

[DATA EXSPOILERS!]
Classified Material

Judging by the fact that "Nibbanic" refers to the Buddhist Nirvana, and these eggs are "Post-Nibbanic", plus the illustrations on 1564-5, the eggs are what happens to humans as an eventual result of exposure to SCP-217. The cultists are, essentially, in their equivalent of Nirvana, or something beyond Nirvana, where they can eventually mesh with the Broken God… if they haven't already, as Nibbana is essentially merging with the supreme being (in Buddhism, the Brahma, in this case, the BG).

Also, each egg is someone significant within the Church. I don't know how they were significant, exactly, other than that -1 is a warrior that fought for the church in some way.

Bleh. I dunno. It sounded smarter before I started typing.

Also, may I just say that I love this particular line:

Should any reliable means of blocking or negating telepathic communications be developed, testing with SCP-1564 is recommended.

Take that, Telekill.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

I really like the relationship here between the SCP and the Church. The Church was apparently keeping them contained for some reason. Now that the Foundation has taken over, nothing has actually changed, except the jailers. These clearly aren't a dangerous Church artifact, as the Church is assisting with containment of -6. Now, they might not be doing it voluntarily, and might be coerced through repeat use of powerful amnestics, but they're still allowed in contact with it, which is something that would just never go with any of the other contained Church artifacts.

This is an article that raises a lot of questions, but not the kind of questions that get raised by sloppy writing. This causes insight and introspection. What is this? Why was the Church keeping it contained? If it's something dangerous to the Church, then doesn't simple 'enemy of my enemy is my friend' mean that this is good? Is the Foundation being reactionary and highly conservative by containing a potential ally? Or maybe the plurality of threats to reality is absolute: The Church is a mad force bent on destruction, but maybe this is too. Was the containment decision made consciously?

The idea of a mechanical theology, of there being schisms within the Church to match the actual tempestuous history of the Christian faith, with reformations and heresy and apostasy within the Church and maybe these things were what originally formed the Church, but got overthrown by the madness of the grinding gears. Of course, the implication that the Church is founded on real Truth, and not on a few men going mad due the relentless grinding. Maybe God exists, and he is clockwork.


tl:dr: Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
3. Containment procedures were devised based on Church of the Broken God documents, information provided by CotBG adherents, and the circumstances of recovery for each instance.

The Foundation doesn't necessarily know why they work. They just know how the CotBG was dealing with them, what members who they interrogated said about them, and things the CotBG has written about them. They deduced Containment Procedures from these.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're providing hugely complex containment procedures, and then not explaining why they're necessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is my attempt to flesh out the CotBG beyond clockwork-worshippers.

If the backstory implied here isn't your piece of cake, there's not much I can do about that, but if you have any suggestions or comments on the writing, just let me know :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is awesome.
